2w • Edited •  
2 weeks ago • Edited • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn  
Saturday. Midnight. I'm in a beanbag in the living room coding for [Kindora](https://www.linkedin.com/company/kindora-pbc/). [Sally](https://www.linkedin.com/in/steelesally/)'s in the rocking chair by the fireplace doing [Outdoorithm](https://www.linkedin.com/company/outdoorithm/) work. This is our life right now—two entrepreneurs grinding after four daughters finally fall asleep and trying to make sense of everything going on in the world.

She turns to me: "I've decided to get back into preaching and writing. I need a place where people can find me and engage with the work."

I said: "I can build you a site for that. Just send me your sermon notes."

By the end of the Seahawks game Sunday, the site was done.

Here's how. Because I want you to try this.

I used Claude Code and something called the Ralph Wiggum Loop. Named after the Simpsons character. Perpetually confused, always making mistakes, but never stops trying. That's the whole technique. You give Claude a task. It works. It tries to quit. The loop catches it and says: not yet. Keep going. The AI sees what it just built and makes it better. Again and again until it's actually done.

Iteration beats single shot. That's the philosophy.

I ran three loops at once. One researched 20+ ministry websites looking for best practices. Another read all of Sally's sermons and LinkedIn posts to learn her voice. The third took those outputs and built the site.

Sunday morning I set the loops running and went for a jog. Up Park Boulevard, past Joaquin Miller, over the Oakmore hills, back down to Glenview. Slow. I'm old. But I have to move my body when I code all day.

Got back. Two loops done. Off to the races.

Turned on the NFL games. Kept pushing Claude forward while watching. This is how I work now. Multiple projects running, and while one compiles I'm directing another. The game becomes background music to building.

Then something wild. I deployed the site locally and connected Claude to a Chrome extension. Told it to visit the actual site. Like a real user. Click through every page and tell me what looked wrong. It found contrast issues, photos placed badly, spacing problems. Made a list of everything.

Another loop to fix them all.

One final pass together. About an hour of editing to lock in Sally's voice. Launch.

I'm an engineer, not a developer. But coding with AI as my pair programmer has collapsed the distance between "I have an idea" and "it exists."

The site captures what I've watched Sally do for years: create spaces, in sanctuaries and around campfires, where people who've been told they don't belong can finally exhale. Now that work has a home: [www.sallysteele.org](http://www.sallysteele.org/)

Saturday midnight to Sunday evening. From a fireplace conversation to a finished site. Seahawks headed to the Super Bowl.

Good Sunday.

What are you building?

[Justin Steele](https://www.linkedin.com/in/justinrichardsteele?miniProfileUrn=urn%3Ali%3Afsd_profile%3AACoAAAAmZkQB2oiK4JBNyhF3LYpDzK4FDJxnyHM)

23h •  
23 hours ago • Visible to anyone on or off LinkedIn  
Tuesday afternoon. Atlanta Tech Village. Twenty [Camelback Ventures](https://www.linkedin.com/company/camelback-ventures/) founders spread across couches, bamboo floors, floor-to-ceiling windows. One conversation was about to send me back to my hotel room to code until 1:30am.

The talk turned to fundraising. How do you approach a funder at a conference? What do you actually say?

I mentioned [Kindora](https://www.linkedin.com/company/kindora-pbc/) already generates a conversation template for each funder brief. But as I said it, a bigger idea formed: What if you could talk to an AI that responds like a real program officer and get coached on how you did?

My co-founder [Karibu](https://www.linkedin.com/in/karibu/) and I had discussed this months ago, but the voice APIs weren't good enough. I searched again that afternoon. OpenAI had released a realtime voice API that I'd missed.

That night, after going 10-5 and 10-6 against a couple fellows at Pop-A-Shot (but who's counting 😛 ) and sharing pizza at Painted Pin, I went back to my room at the Kimpton Buckhead. Filled my water bottle with spa water from the lobby. Popped my laptop open at 9pm.

By 1:30am: a fully functional AI voice practice tool. Pick any funder from Kindora's database of 175,000+ foundations. Choose your scenario. Start talking. It responds in a persona built from real funder data, foundation profiles, and everything I learned giving away $700M at [Google.org](http://google.org/) about how funders actually talk to nonprofits. It coaches you across eight dimensions.

Barely slept, too excited. Up at 7:30am.

That afternoon at the Russell Innovation Center for Entrepreneurs, I demoed it live in front of 19 peers, pitching the Cliff Family Foundation, a funder my wife Sally and I are pursuing for our nonprofit Outdoorithm Collective. The AI responded in real time, kicked my butt, gave me feedback.

[Brianna Baker](https://www.linkedin.com/in/brianna-baker-30850a1b2/) from Justice for Black Girls lit up about training her young people to fundraise for their own initiatives. Others expressed the same.

This is what we do at Kindora. I spent too long paying $300–$500/mo for fundraising platforms with annual contracts that drowned me in irrelevant matches. That prices out 85% of nonprofits, disproportionately the ones closest to communities they serve. We sit with those leaders. We listen. And we build fast.

This week with Camelback Cohort 16 gave me clarity. Nineteen other founders doing hard, necessary work. We laughed. We sang. We danced. We sharpened each other's thinking. I loved every minute.

The product is ready. Time to get it into the hands of the leaders who need it.

If you lead a nonprofit and have struggled with what to say to a funder, Kindora is free to start. No annual contract. You get credits for pitch practice, funder intel briefs, and AI prospecting from 175,000+ foundations and 5.7M grants. On day one.

Grateful to Camelback Ventures for the kind of space where underrepresented founders thrive.
